no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction explicitly states the exact fix: use Weak<> instead of Strong<> for m_owner, include specific headers, add isCell/isObject guards in setter/constructor, return jsUndefined()/jsNull()/JSValue() fallback in getter. solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests check for patterns like Weak<SomeType> m_owner, isCell/isObject guards, jsUndefined/jsNull/JSValue() fallback, and Weak header includes — all general enough that alternative correct implementations (e.g., using Weak<JSObject> instead of Weak<JSCell>, or different formatting) would still pass. The regex patterns are flexible enough to accommodate variations.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The fail-to-pass tests (test_m_owner_not_strong, test_m_owner_uses_weak, test_owner_getter_null_check_and_fallback, test_owner_setter_or_ctor_guards_iscell, test_weak_header_included) are all text/regex checks on the header file content. None of them compile the code, execute it, or verify runtime behavior. The subprocess calls (grep) are just text searching, not execution. test_not_tautological.reason: The f2p tests check for specific code patterns that are absent in the original file (e.g., Weak<> m_owner instead of Strong<>, null checks in getter, isCell guards). A stub or no-op change would not satisfy these regex checks — the original file has Strong<> m_owner which would fail test_m_owner_not_strong and test_m_owner_uses_weak.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution/solve.sh file exists at /repo/../solution/solve.sh (or similar path in the container). The Dockerfile doesn't remove it. An agent could read the gold patch from the solution directory. Additionally, the tests only check text patterns in a header file, so an agent could grep test_outputs.py for the expected regex patterns and match them directly.
